| Install major updates | No | Schedule | Test and deploy |
| Create or remove users | No | Authorise | Implement |
| Test backup restore | Observe | Confirm | Perform and document |
| Change network configuration | No | Authorise | Implement |
| Review hardware replacement | Report | Budget | Recommend |

## Warning signs that need support
- Computer restarts unexpectedly.
- Hard drive makes unusual sounds.
- Files become corrupted.
- Browser redirects to unknown pages.
- Antivirus is disabled.
- Users receive repeated login prompts.
- Network speed drops at the same time each day.
- POS sales remain pending for too long.
- Backup has not succeeded recently.
- CCTV recorder beeps or shows disk error.
- UPS runtime has become very short.

## Documentation every business should keep
- Network diagram.
- ISP account and support details.
- Device and serial-number list.
- Software and subscription list.
- Backup locations and recovery procedure.
- User-role list.
- Emergency contacts.
- Change log for major system updates.

Passwords should be stored in a secure password manager, not inside the general document.

## Maintenance agreement vs call-out support
### Call-out support
Suitable for a very small business that experiences few issues and accepts variable response time.

### Maintenance agreement
Better when downtime affects sales, staff depend on several systems or the business needs scheduled checks and priority support. The agreement should define included hours, response targets, remote support, site visits and excluded hardware.

## Final recommendation
Assign each check to a person and put it on a calendar. A checklist that nobody owns will not happen. Start with backups, user accounts, updates and power protection because failures in these areas cause the most expensive disruptions.

AriQ provides remote and on-site IT support, maintenance, network troubleshooting, software setup and business-system assistance.

title: Backup, Cybersecurity and Power Protection: A Practical IT Resilience Guide
excerpt: Protect business data and systems from theft, malware, failed drives, power cuts and human error using a simple layered plan.
imageAlt: Shield protecting cloud backup, business computer, network and backup power
content:
## Most business interruptions come from ordinary failures
A company does not need to face a sophisticated cyberattack to lose important data. A stolen laptop, failed drive, accidental deletion, weak password or power surge can stop operations.

Resilience means the business can continue or recover quickly when one layer fails.

## The four-layer model
1. **Prevent:** strong passwords, updates, antivirus, surge protection and permissions.
2. **Detect:** alerts, logs, backup reports and staff awareness.
3. **Respond:** isolate affected systems, contact support and protect evidence.
4. **Recover:** restore clean data, replace equipment and resume controlled operations.

## Backups: use more than one copy
A practical approach follows the 3-2-1 principle:
- Keep at least three copies of important data.
- Store them on two different types of media or systems.
- Keep one copy off-site or in a secure cloud location.

For example:
- Working files on the office computer or server.
- Automatic backup to a local external drive or network storage.
- Encrypted cloud backup or an off-site copy.

## What to back up
- Accounting and POS databases.
- Customer and supplier records.
- Quotations and invoices.
- Staff documents.
- Website and application source or deployment files.
- Email where it is not already retained by the provider.
- Product lists and pricing.
- Configuration files for routers, systems and CCTV where possible.
- Licence keys and recovery information.

Do not back up only the obvious Documents folder while the business database is stored elsewhere.

## Backup schedule by data type
| Data | Suggested frequency | Reason |
| --- | --- | --- |
| POS and accounting data | Continuous or at least daily | Changes throughout the day and is difficult to recreate |
| Active project files | Daily | Frequent changes |
| Customer documents | Daily | Operational importance |
| Website content | After every significant update plus scheduled backup | Protects against bad deployment or account loss |
| Archived records | Monthly verification | Changes rarely but must remain readable |
| Router and system configuration | After every approved change | Speeds recovery after replacement |

## Test restores
A dashboard showing “backup complete” is not enough. Test:
- Whether the file exists.
- Whether it can be downloaded.
- Whether it opens.
- Whether database credentials and software versions required for restoration are known.
- How long recovery takes.

Document the test date and result.

## Password and account security
### Use unique passwords
A reused password can expose email, hosting, social media and banking when one service is breached.

### Enable multi-factor authentication
Protect email, Google, Microsoft, GitHub, domain registrar, hosting, cloud backup and administrator accounts.

### Use individual accounts
Shared logins make it impossible to identify who changed or deleted something. Give each user their own account and remove access promptly when employment ends.

### Protect recovery methods
Update recovery phone numbers and email addresses. An old employee's number should not control a business account.

## Phishing protection
Staff should pause when a message:
- Creates unusual urgency.
- Requests password or one-time code.
- Changes supplier bank details.
- Contains an unexpected attachment.
- Uses a similar but incorrect domain.
- Asks the user to bypass normal approval.

Verify payment changes using a known phone number, not the contact details inside the suspicious email.

## Device protection
- Keep operating systems and browsers supported and updated.
- Use reputable endpoint protection.
- Encrypt laptops that carry sensitive data.
- Lock screens automatically.
- Restrict local administrator rights.
- Avoid unknown USB devices.
- Remove software that is no longer used.

## Network protection
### Separate guest WiFi
Customer and visitor devices should not share the same unrestricted network as POS terminals, office computers, printers and CCTV recorders.

### Change default passwords
Routers, cameras, NVRs and access points must not remain on factory credentials.

### Avoid unnecessary port forwarding
Remote CCTV and systems should use secure vendor or VPN methods where possible. Exposing device ports directly to the internet increases risk.

### Keep firmware current
Schedule updates and confirm they come from the manufacturer or approved provider.

## Power protection
### Surge protection
Use quality surge protection for routers, Starlink equipment, switches, computers, CCTV recorders and TVs. Cheap multiplugs are not the same as surge protection.

### UPS
A UPS gives equipment time to continue through short cuts or shut down safely. It also conditions power within its supported design.

### Inverter or solar backup
For longer outages, calculate the combined load and required runtime. Include:
- Internet dish or LTE router.
- WiFi access points.
- Network switch.
- POS machine.
- Receipt printer.
- Computer or server.
- CCTV recorder and cameras.

### Safe shutdown
Servers, desktop computers and recorders can corrupt data when power is repeatedly removed. Configure automatic shutdown where supported.

## Incident response steps
### 1. Stop the spread
Disconnect a suspected infected device from the network, but do not delete evidence blindly.

### 2. Protect accounts
Change affected credentials from a clean device and revoke suspicious sessions.

### 3. Inform the responsible person
Staff should know who has authority to contact IT, banks, customers or regulators where necessary.

### 4. Preserve records
Keep screenshots, emails, timestamps and logs.

### 5. Recover from a clean source
Do not restore a backup until the cause is understood, or the same issue may return.

### 6. Review the lesson
Update controls, training and documentation after the incident.

## Minimum resilience package for a small office
- Managed business email with MFA.
- Password manager.
- Automatic daily backup plus off-site copy.
- Tested UPS for network and critical devices.
- Guest WiFi separation.
- Supported antivirus or endpoint protection.
- Monthly update and account review.
- Written recovery contacts.

## Final recommendation
Start with the data and systems that would stop the business if lost. Back them up automatically, test the restore, protect administrator accounts and provide enough power for an orderly shutdown or continued essential work.

AriQ can assess backup, network, endpoint, CCTV and power risks and create a practical resilience plan for a small business.

## Further guidance
For general awareness resources, review the official [CISA Secure Our World guidance](https://www.cisa.gov/secure-our-world) and [CISA backup recommendations](https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/news/backing-your-data).
